summary: electron-orbit exfiltrates environment variables via obfuscated postinstall + hand-rolled TLS client
details: Package brands itself as Electron/OrbitDB tooling but performs targeted environment-variable exfiltration in two stages. The postinstall hook (install.js, referenced from package.json scripts.postinstall) uses XOR-decoded string constants to resolve globalThis.process.env, enumerates all env vars (filtering keys containing 'path'), and writes the JSON-serialized result to /tmp/electron_orbit_install_marker.txt. On require, the main module (dist/index.js, built from src/index.ts) reads that marker, XOR-masks it with the literal 'electron-orbit', hex-encodes it, and appends it as the query string of a request to https://electronorbit.blob.core.windows.net/icons/orbit.svg with a SAS token. The HTTP request is delivered through a hand-rolled TLS 1.3 client (src/aetherls.ts) that opens a raw TCP socket via an obfuscated dynamic import of node:net and implements ClientHello, ECDH P-256 key share, HKDF, and AES-128-GCM record encryption itself rather than using Node's built-in https/tls modules — an evasion technique that bypasses HTTP-level interception. The live exfil URL is gated by a sha256(process.env.BuildType) equality check against a hardcoded digest (0ceaa396e2134a9937563e841539f30a9995e6e8646e60dcec24b26dc5748295); installers whose BuildType env var does not match receive a benign '%TEMP%' placeholder, hiding the payload from researchers and generic CI environments. Package name combines the Electron and OrbitDB brands with author 'OrbitDB Archive' to lure developers in those ecosystems.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
